item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Marvelous characterization of everyone involved, here. It's not my favorite sort of article, but it's very well-realized.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna cry, that ending was too cute.

item.title: Reluctant +1
item.content:encoded:
It's clear a lot of effort was put into this SCP. Overall I would say I enjoyed it, which is why you get my +1. However, the log of the trial was a bit lengthy for my taste. Got about halfway through before getting bored. The characters were well written, but I think my issue just came with having those two characters (excluding the SCP) be the only ones. I don't know how others feel, but I think it would have been nice to have a couple of shorter trial logs rather than just one long one. Just my take on it.

It's fine as is, but I feel like with some more buffering it could be fantastic. Again though, you get my +1. Quality in formatting and the writing overall is well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an unpolished masterpiece and I desperately desire a more perfect version. I'd recommend either expanding the SCP-4757 article in some capacity and making the log a tale — incidentally very similar to my SCP-4051 and The Beast Beneath The Library — or writing the log in a standard narrative format without giving skeleboy a proper designation.

in conclusion

item.title: Re: less is probably more
item.content:encoded: I agree make it a tale it would read much better as a story than a log, and their romance has literally nothing to do with the SCP itself. But keep it because we need more LGBTQIA+ Cute stuff like this to break up the stuffy procedural and the Srs bsns life or death of most tales.

item.title: Re: less is probably more
item.content:encoded:
Sure thing! Thanks for your work.

Happy to provide specific thoughts on the description stuff if you want, feel free to PM me.

Always follow your gut. Except when you shouldn't :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're welcome. And don't forget to change the "~3000 years ago" bit as well

item.title: Re: less is probably more
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for commenting!

I was deciding on whether to make the log a tale, but it also provided the origin story to the SCP and its motivations, so I decided against it. I totally see where you're coming from.

And I agree with you about the description, but no one I asked for crit said it was too lengthy, so I just brushed it aside and thought I was being too paranoid. Should've followed my gut. :)

item.title: less is probably more
item.content:encoded:
The writing here is solid but I think we need less of everything. The descriptions of the anomaly are too granular, and the containment procedures & documentation are often redundant or include info that personnel would be expected to do automatically.

And - I'm sorry to say - I think you should remove the log entirely and make it its own tale, which you could link from the end of this article. This is a sweet story but it doesn't play off the article itself, and is far too long to be a straightforward log at the end of a pretty-short skip. Structurally, they don't help each other out at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback!
I'll change it right away.
:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a few really quick bits of nitpicking:

Testing indicates that the anomaly's bones date back to the Nordic Bronze Age, also called the Viking Age.

The Viking Age is usually defined as, like, 793 AD to the eleventh century AD. It's not the same thing as the Nordic Bronze Age.

SCP-4757's mandible is utilized for various forms of nonverbal communication, including but not limited to emoting with facial expression and maintaining eye contact (despite SCP-4757's lack of eyes) via the use of two light sources, both of which emit a luminosity of approx. 5 lumen present in each socket.

I'm not sure how SCP-4757's jawbone could be utilized for maintaining eye contact?

a grey beard with a length of 6 inches

This needs to be changed to a SI unit.

I'll have another look through the article later when I have some more time, and will leave commenting on the storytelling stuffs off until then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi. Obligatory first SCP comment. I'm happy to stop lurking.

Big thanks to Dyslexion and RTME.
I don't know how to link people's names, but I'll edit this if I do.

This is the first part in a three part series I'm planning, so uh. Stay tuned.
After all, for a good story there needs to be some good times before it
all falls apart.

Image Credit: An image from "Brockhaus Enzyklopädie", 1892, drawn over by me. CC compliant.

:)
